variables.φ: Phase angle between voltage and current (rad)

locked_facts: Impedance: Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²), where X_L = ωL and X_C = 1/(ωC)
Resonance when X_L = X_C, i.e., ω₀ = 1/√(LC) — impedance is minimum (Z = R), current is maximum
Phase angle: tan(φ) = (X_L - X_C)/R; current leads voltage when X_C > X_L (capacitive), lags when X_L > X_C (inductive)
Power factor: cos(φ) = R/Z — fraction of apparent power that does useful work
Quality factor: Q = ω₀L/R = 1/(ω₀CR) = (1/R)√(L/C) — sharpness of resonance peak
At resonance: V_L = V_C (equal and opposite), V_R = V_source, current is maximum I₀ = V₀/R
truth_statements: In a series LCR circuit, the impedance depends on R, L, C, and the frequency of the AC source
At resonance frequency, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, leaving only resistance to oppose current
The power factor cos(φ) determines how much of the supplied power is actually consumed as useful work
Higher Q factor means sharper resonance — the circuit is more selective about which frequencies pass through
The phasor diagram shows V_R in phase with I, V_L leading I by 90°, and V_C lagging I by 90°

aha_moment.what_to_show: Frequency sweep from below resonance to above resonance. Below resonance: X_C > X_L, circuit is capacitive, current leads voltage. At resonance: X_L = X_C cancel, Z = R (minimum), current jumps to maximum, phasor V_L and V_C are equal and opposite. Above resonance: X_L > X_C, inductive, current lags.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Current vs frequency graph peaks sharply at ω₀. Phasor diagram rotates from capacitive to resistive to inductive. Impedance dips to R at resonance.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that V_L and V_C can each be LARGER than V_source (Q amplification) yet they cancel at resonance — voltage across L or C individually can exceed source voltage

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Series LCR circuit with AC source. R, L, C values labeled. Phasor diagram empty. Current and voltage waveforms ready to plot. X_L, X_C, Z formulas displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Three elements in series: R (dissipates), L (stores magnetic energy), C (stores electric energy)

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: AC source turned on at a frequency below resonance. Current and voltage waveforms plotted — current leads voltage (capacitive regime). Phasor diagram: V_C > V_L. Impedance Z calculated and shown larger than R.
simulation_states.emphasis: Below resonance: X_C > X_L, so the net reactance is capacitive, current leads voltage

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Frequency tuned to resonance: ω = 1/√(LC). X_L = X_C — they cancel perfectly. Z drops to R. Current peaks at V₀/R. Phasor: V_L and V_C are equal, opposite, and cancel. Only V_R remains = V_source. Power factor = 1.
simulation_states.emphasis: At resonance: L and C reactances CANCEL. Maximum current. Maximum power transfer. cos(φ) = 1.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust frequency, R, L, C values. Watch impedance, current, phase angle, and phasor diagram update. Sweep frequency to trace the resonance curve. Adjust R to change Q factor (sharpness).
simulation_states.emphasis: Lower R → higher Q → sharper resonance peak. Change L or C to shift resonance frequency.

common_misconceptions.belief: At resonance, no current flows because L and C cancel each other out
trigger_phrases: L aur C cancel toh current kaise
cancel hone se kuch nahi bachega
common_misconceptions.correction: At resonance, the REACTANCES (X_L and X_C) cancel — not the components themselves. This leaves only R opposing the current, so current is MAXIMUM (I = V/R), not zero. L and C still store and exchange energy, but their voltage drops cancel in the series loop.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show current peaking at resonance on the I-f graph. Z drops to R. Current is V₀/R — the maximum possible.

common_misconceptions.belief: The voltage across L or C cannot exceed the source voltage
trigger_phrases: inductor pe source se zyada voltage kaise
more voltage than supply not possible
common_misconceptions.correction: At resonance, V_L = QV_source and V_C = QV_source. Since Q can be much larger than 1 (Q = 10, 50, or even 100), the voltage across L or C can be many times the source voltage. They cancel each other, but individually they are amplified.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show V_L and V_C individually much larger than V_source at resonance, but their phasors are opposite and cancel

legend.meaning: Inductor — stores energy in magnetic field
Capacitor — stores energy in electric field
V_R (along I), V_L (90° ahead), V_C (90° behind)
